Model Architecture Category: MLP-based / Linear model (per-channel or shared linear projection)
Core Mechanism & Structure: A simple normalization-then-linear extrapolation model: it subtracts the last observed value per channel (a last-value centering step), applies a learned linear map from past window length (seq_len) to forecast horizon (pred_len) either shared across channels or independently per channel, then adds back the last value to recover the absolute level. No encoder-decoder stack, no attention, no recurrence, and no convolution; forecasting is performed via direct linear projection along the time dimension.
Core Innovations: 1) Last-value centering (subtracting x[t_last]) to reduce level/non-stationarity effects before linear projection, then re-adding it afterward. 2) Optional per-variable (per-channel) independent linear heads vs. a shared linear head across all channels, enabling either parameter sharing or channel-specific dynamics. 3) Direct seq_len→pred_len linear mapping for multi-step output in one shot (no autoregressive decoding).

Key Technical Details.Input Embedding: None. Raw input tensor x of shape [B, L, C] is used directly; the model only permutes dimensions when using a shared linear layer.
Key Technical Details.Feature Extraction: Pure linear projection over the time axis: nn.Linear(seq_len, pred_len) applied either (a) per channel via ModuleList of independent Linear layers, or (b) shared by permuting to [B, C, L], applying one Linear, then permuting back.
Key Technical Details.Positional Encoding: No positional encoding (the linear layer weights implicitly learn time-position contributions within the fixed seq_len window).
Key Technical Details.Non-stationarity Handling: Deterministic last-value centering: subtracts the final timestep (per channel) before projection and adds it back after; uses detach() on the last value to stop gradients through the reference level.

Direct Observations.claim: Implements a linear seq_len→pred_len forecasting head (no attention/encoder/decoder).
Direct Observations.evidence: models/NLinear.py: __init__ defines self.Linear = nn.Linear(self.seq_len, self.pred_len) or per-channel ModuleList of nn.Linear(self.seq_len, self.pred_len); forward applies self.Linear to produce [Batch, pred_len, Channel].

Direct Observations.claim: Applies last-timestep centering and then restores the level (a simple normalization strategy).
Direct Observations.evidence: models/NLinear.py forward: seq_last = x[:,-1:,:].detach(); x = x - seq_last; ...; x = x + seq_last

Direct Observations.claim: Supports per-channel independent linear layers controlled by configs.individual.
Direct Observations.evidence: models/NLinear.py: if self.individual: self.Linear = nn.ModuleList(); for i in range(self.channels): self.Linear.append(nn.Linear(self.seq_len,self.pred_len)); forward loops output[:,:,i] = self.Linear[i](x[:,:,i])

Direct Observations.claim: Shared linear layer case mixes no information across channels (applies same temporal projection to each channel).
Direct Observations.evidence: models/NLinear.py forward (else branch): x = self.Linear(x.permute(0,2,1)).permute(0,2,1) — Linear is applied on last dimension (time), with channels as batch-like dimension.

Direct Observations.claim: Stops gradients through the reference last value used for centering.

Unknown / Not in Code: Any attention mechanism (full/sparse/linear attention) is not present.
Any explicit encoder-decoder architecture is not present.
Any positional/time-feature embeddings are not present.
Any series decomposition, RevIN, LayerNorm/BatchNorm, or frequency-domain modules are not present.

Strengths.label: Very fast and memory-efficient forecasting baseline
Strengths.why: Only uses one (or C) small linear layers; computation is a single matrix multiply per channel with no quadratic-time components.
Strengths.conditions: When seq_len and pred_len are moderate and you need high throughput (e.g., many series, large batch sizes).

Strengths.label: Works well when dynamics are approximately linear after de-meaning by last value
Strengths.why: Last-value centering removes level shifts; the remaining residual can be modeled by a linear mapping from history to future.
Strengths.conditions: Series with strong local continuity/level shifts but relatively simple temporal patterns (e.g., short-term trends, smooth signals).

Strengths.label: Robustness to level offsets / local non-stationarity
Strengths.why: Subtracting the last observed value makes the model focus on changes relative to the most recent level rather than absolute scale.
Strengths.conditions: When series exhibit varying baselines across windows (e.g., drifting mean) and you use sliding windows.

Weaknesses.label: Limited capacity for nonlinear or regime-switching dynamics
Weaknesses.why: The core mapping is purely linear with no nonlinear feature extractor, attention, or deep stack.
Weaknesses.conditions: Complex seasonality, sharp events, nonlinear interactions, or multiscale patterns requiring hierarchical modeling.

Weaknesses.label: No explicit cross-channel interaction (unless you rely on independent heads only)
Weaknesses.why: Both modes apply temporal projection per channel; neither mode mixes information across different channels/variables.
Weaknesses.conditions: Multivariate forecasting where variables are strongly coupled and cross-variable dependencies are important.

Weaknesses.label: Fixed-window dependence and potential sensitivity to seq_len choice
Weaknesses.why: Uses a fixed nn.Linear over the seq_len positions; it cannot adaptively attend beyond the chosen window.
Weaknesses.conditions: When long-range dependencies exceed seq_len or when optimal receptive field varies over time.

Resource Requirements & Complexity: Parameter count is small: shared mode has ~pred_len*seq_len (+bias) parameters; individual mode has ~C*pred_len*seq_len (+biases) parameters. Compute is O(B*C*seq_len*pred_len) for a forward pass (essentially dense GEMMs). Memory footprint is minimal compared to deep sequence models; no O(L^2) attention matrices.
